Anchoring on humps and points and fishing with cut bait at a variety of depths is the best bet. As the spawn mostly passes, fishing should improve for both big fish and numbers of fish. [Most detailed Lake Thurmond Updates](https://www.anglersheadquarters.com/pages/clarks-hill-lake-thurmond-fishing-report) ### Lake Wylie **Bass bass:** Tournament angler Reid McGinn of Fort Mill reports that during July one group of fish will be shallow feeding around bream, and another group of fish will be offshore in well-known community holes. Shallow fish can be caught on topwaters or soft plastics, and deeper fish can be caught on crankbaits and worms. **Crappie adn white perch:** Captain Chris Nichols (704-860-7951) reports that again this month the two species will often be mixed together, commonly at the mouths of the creeks or on main lake flats. The white perch will be in massive schools that often blanket the whole water column, while crappie spread out more. Minnow rigs are often the best way to target both species but you can also troll crankbaits or jigs for crappie – and other species. **Catfish:** Captain Rodger Taylor (803-517-7828) reports that during the day drifting mid-depths with cut bluegill is a good option, while at night anchoring with cut bait and fan-casting to a variety of depths can be the best pattern. The bite should pick up once the catfish spawn completely wraps up, even though it never slowed much in June. [Most detailed Lake Wylie Updates](https://www.anglersheadquarters.com/pages/lake-wylie-fishing-report) ## Midlands Area ### Lake Greenwood **Black bass:** Veteran tournament angler Stan Gunter of Greenwood reports that in July there should be a topwater bite first thing in shallow water, but during the heat of the day fish should be caught deep on worms around points and brush. There should also still be numbers of spotted bass around deeper docks. **Crappie:** Captain Roland Addy (864-980-3672) reports that in July most fish should set up on main lake brush and leave the creeks. At first they will continue to take jigs, but as they get more finicky minnows may produce better. Most of the fish will be suspended in deep water, but factors like sun and cloud cover, water clarity, and cool weather can move them up and down the water column or even move them shallower. **Catfish:** Captain Chris Simpson reports that channel catfish can be caught drifting cut bait or anchoring with dip baits. Flathead catfish can be caught on live bait at night. [Most detailed Lake Greenwood Updates](https://www.anglersheadquarters.com/pages/lake-greenwood-fishing-report) ### Lake Monticello **Black bass:** BYA Fishing’s Eric Enlow of Union reports that in July most of the fish will be in deeper water off points and humps, and soft plastics, flutter spoons, or big crankbaits will be the best way to catch them. In late June the bite was excellent, but as the month goes on the fish may get more finicky and finesse tactics may become important. In July there can still be a topwater bite in the shallows early or at night. Overall, the fishing is usually better when there is current. **Catfish:** Captain William Attaway (803-924-0857) reports that in July numbers of fish will be caught free-line drifting over deep water, while bigger fish can be caught anchoring cut bait on humps. This is a better month for catching lots of fish than big ones. [Most detailed Lake Monticello Updates](https://www.anglersheadquarters.com/pages/lake-monticello-fishing-report) ### Lake Murray **Black Bass:** Tournament angler Andy Wicker of Pomaria reports that in July there can be a good shallow buzzbait bite early, but you often have to fish deep brush during the day (and at night). Some fish will also be suspended offshore and feed upwards on topwater lures in the heat of summer. The rivers offer an option where shallow cover can be fishable even during the day, and the bite can be particularly good if rain creates some current. **Striped bass:** Captain Brad Taylor (803-331-1354) reports that in July most fish should be in deeper water in the lower pool and at least in sight of the dam, and by the end of June most were already moving that way. This month fish will generally be caught on down-lined live herring – but other patterns could develop depending on weather and water conditions. **Crappie:** Captain Brad Taylor reports that fish will continue to hold around brush in July, but in different parts of the lake you will need to fish different depths. In the upper lake you can fish shallower, while in the lower lake deeper brush closer to the main body of water will hold the most and biggest fish. Jigs can work throughout the day in the rivers, but in the lower lake minnows are often more successful – especially outside of very early or late. **Catfish:** Captain William Attaway (803-924-0857) reports that both because of feeding patterns and boat traffic the night bite will be much better during July. Dip baits and a variety of cut bait will catch fish in shallow water, and long points and humps are both good places to look. [Most detailed Lake Murray Updates](https://www.anglersheadquarters.com/pages/lake-murray-fishing-report) ### Lake Wateree **Black bass:** Tournament angler Dearal Rodgers of Camden reports that often in July deeper patterns are more effective for Wateree bass. Shaky heads, jigs, big crankbaits, and Carolina rigs will all catch fish around offshore depth changes, but in the summer heat you may have to throw everything at them before seeing what the bass will eat. You can also target deeper docks with worms or jigs, and early in the morning there may continue to be a topwater bite off main lake points. **Striped bass:** Captain Justin Whiteside (803-417-0070) reports that striper fishing at Lake Wateree is very reliable during July, as most of the fish congregate in the lower half of the lake. He locates schools using electronics and fishes with downlines and freelines. He suggests keeping a topwater rig handy as surface schooling becomes consistent during July. **Crappie:** Veteran tournament angler Will Hinson of Cassatt reports that in July fish will be on brush in the main river channel, and his preferred method will be casting Fish Stalker Lures. Minnows will also work. Fish will also be found around deeper bridges but otherwise avoid the creeks. **Catfish:** Captain Rodger Taylor (803-517-7828) reports that in July drifting in the mid-lake area with cut bait is usually the best way to catch numbers of catfish. More big fish should show up again as the spawn wraps up. [Most detailed Lake Wateree Updates](https://www.anglersheadquarters.com/pages/lake-wateree-fishing-report) ### Santee Cooper **Black bass:** Captain David Murdaugh jSr. (843-452-9566) reports that early there can be a topwater bite around lily pads and shallow vegetation, but during the day the best action will often be around eel grass and hydrilla beds or offshore brush and ledges. Big worms, jerkbaits and crankbaits can all work. **Crappie:** Captain Steve English (843-729-4044) reports that this month fish should continue to be caught on mid-depth brush with minnows, but as it gets very hot they could move a little deeper. It’s been a fair year for crappie and they hope to be able to continue to catch a decent number of fish in July. **Bream:** Captain Steve English (843-729-4044) reports that around the new and full moons waves of bluegill and some shellcracker should come shallow to spawn where they can be caught on crickets and worms. In between the fish are supposed to hold around the first break, but this year they have often been hard to locate. Smaller bream are easy to find on brush piles. **Catfish:** Captain Stevie English (843-709-8138) and Captain Bobby Winters (843-751-3080) report that to start July the best fishing is usually drifting or anchoring in relatively shallow water in less than 20 feet, but at some point the bigger fish may go deeper – although at night they can come shallow again. White perch and bream are usually good summer baits. **Upper Lake Marion:** : (note: recognizing the uniqueness of this section of the lake, we will try to offer a report from the top of the upper lake whenever possible going forward): Relaxed Retreat (803-478-2800), located in Jacks Creek and the I-95 Bridge area, reports that the ecosystem at the top of the upper lake supports some very good fishing throughout July. One of their black bass guides Captain Rodney Reed (also available at 803-825-9144) reports that largemouth bass in upper Lake Marion will be holding in 5-8 feet of water during the day around the plentiful woody and weedy cover in this section of the lake, while the shallow-water topwater bite will be good early and late in the day throughout July. Crappie will be on deeper brush but biting well, while bream will be in 3-7 feet of water around weed edges and cypress tree clusters. Sporadic bedding will occur all month in shallow water. The July catfish patterns at the top of Santee typically involve fishing shallow flats less than 6 feet deep early and late, then drift-fishing deeper water along the channels mid-day. Note that nocturnal catfishing the shallow flats from an anchored setup is very productive on calm nights. [Most detailed Santee Cooper System Updates](https://www.anglersheadquarters.com/pages/santee-cooper-lake-marion-lake-moultrie-fishing-report) ## Mountains Area ### Lake Jocassee **Trout:** Guide Sam Jones (864-280-9056) reports that it’s been a fantastic June, and so they are very optimistic for July. By the end of June fish had already gone very deep, and they expect to find them close to as deep as they will be all year this month. Spoons should work for most of the month but if the bite gets tougher you can try minnows. There can also be a good night bite at the dam with live bait or worms. [Most detailed Lake Jocassee Updates](https://www.anglersheadquarters.com/pages/lake-jocassee-fishing-report) ### Lake Keowee **Black Bass:** Guide Charles Townson (864-324-2065) reports that during July fish will remain in a summer pattern where they can be caught early and late off points on topwaters, while during the day fishing deeper with soft plastics is often the best option unless they are willing to come up to eat. Look out for possible schooling activity this month. [Most detailed Lake Keowee Updates](https://www.anglersheadquarters.com/pages/lake-keowee-fishing-report) ### Lake Hartwell **Black Bass:** Guide Lane Clark (864-254-8614) reports that typically in July some fish will be caught on drop shots and shaky heads around deep brush piles, and fish can also be “called up” to the surface offshore with topwater lures. Sometimes there is a good shallow bite in July around bream, but there are usually more fish offshore. **Striper and hybrids:** Captain Chip Hamilton (864-304-9011) reports that in July there will be a good bite in the river channel itself over deep water near timber. Sometimes fish will be over clean spots, and other times they will suspend in holes in the trees. However, you have to look at both every morning because fish have different preferences on different days. Herring on down-rods are the main pattern. **Crappie:** Guide Rodney Donald (864-356-0143) reports that he expected the fishing to really slow down in June, but the action was still good around a variety of types of structure and so they are optimistic for July. Fish may go deeper this month, although weather and water conditions can combine to surprise anglers. Expect minnows to out-produce jigs in the heat. **Catfish:** Captain Bill Plumley reports that during July channel catfish will bite well in less than 20 feet of water on a variety of baits including cut herring, dip baits, and nightcrawlers. Flatheads can be caught at night on live bait, especially as the spawn winds down. [Most detailed Lake Hartwell updates](https://www.anglersheadquarters.com/pages/lake-hartwell-fishing-report) * * * South Carolina [freshwater recreational fishing regulations](http://www.eregulations.com/southcarolina/huntingandfishing/freshwater-game-fishing-regulations/).

This page lists works of an author who doesn't have a ResearchGate profile or hasn't added the works to their profile yet. It is automatically generated from public (personal) data to further our legitimate goal of comprehensive and accurate scientific recordkeeping. If you are this author and want this page removed, please [let us know](https://www.researchgate.net/contact). ## Publications (8) [The influence of dissolved oxygen on dinoflagellate cyst distribution across Sluice Pond, a meromictic lake in NE Massachusetts, USA](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/316467484_The_influence_of_dissolved_oxygen_on_dinoflagellate_cyst_distribution_across_Sluice_Pond_a_meromictic_lake_in_NE_Massachusetts_USA?_tp=eyJjb250ZXh0Ijp7InBhZ2UiOiJzY2llbnRpZmljQ29udHJpYnV0aW9ucyIsInByZXZpb3VzUGFnZSI6bnVsbCwic3ViUGFnZSI6bnVsbH19) * [Article](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/316467484_The_influence_of_dissolved_oxygen_on_dinoflagellate_cyst_distribution_across_Sluice_Pond_a_meromictic_lake_in_NE_Massachusetts_USA) April 2017 · 291 Reads · 7 Citations Palynology [![Image 1](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/276843270819842-1443015987115_Q64/Francine-Mccarthy.jpg)Francine M G Mccarthy](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Francine-Mccarthy) · [![Image 2](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/435982309481010/images/template/default/author/author_default_m.jpg)Matea Drljepan](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Matea-Drljepan-2039467270) · [![Image 3](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/287888588722183-1445649396139_Q64/J-Hubeny-2.jpg)J. Lakebed sediments below oxygenated waters (DO >2 mg/L) contain diverse testate amoebae, but sparse Peridinium willei-dominated dinoflagellate cyst assemblages. Sediments collected under hypoxic conditions (DO ... According to qualitative studies, the most diverse cyst assemblages are found in sediments from meso-eutrophic lakes with circumneutral pH (Zippi et al., 1990(Zippi et al., , 1991McCarthy et al., 2011 McCarthy et al., , 2017. In the IISD-ELA calibration lakes, the highest diversity of dinoflagellate cysts (75% of the total assemblage in the calibration set) was observed in the more shallow, high nutrient, low-to-circumneutral pH lakes (Fig. 3). ... > > > > Reference: > > [The utility of freshwater dinoflagellate cyst assemblages as a paleoecological proxy: An assessment from boreal lakes (northwest Ontario, Canada)](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/380353398_The_utility_of_freshwater_dinoflagellate_cyst_assemblages_as_a_paleoecological_proxy_An_assessment_from_boreal_lakes_northwest_Ontario_Canada) [The influence of dissolved oxygen on dinoflagellate cyst distribution across Sluice Pond, a meromictic lake in NE Massachusetts, USA](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/316467484_The_influence_of_dissolved_oxygen_on_dinoflagellate_cyst_distribution_across_Sluice_Pond_a_meromictic_lake_in_NE_Massachusetts_USA?_tp=eyJjb250ZXh0Ijp7InBhZ2UiOiJzY2llbnRpZmljQ29udHJpYnV0aW9ucyIsInByZXZpb3VzUGFnZSI6bnVsbCwic3ViUGFnZSI6bnVsbH19) * Citing Article * April 2017 Palynology [![Image 42](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/276843270819842-1443015987115_Q64/Francine-Mccarthy.jpg)Francine M G Mccarthy](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Francine-Mccarthy) · [![Image 43](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/435982309481010/images/template/default/author/author_default_m.jpg)Matea Drljepan](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Matea-Drljepan-2039467270) · [![Image 44](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/287888588722183-1445649396139_Q64/J-Hubeny-2.jpg)J. Bradford Hubeny](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/J-Hubeny-2) · [![Image 49](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/435982309481010/images/template/default/author/author_default_m.jpg)Riddick NJ](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Riddick-NJ-2113755252) > ... A number of studies have been completed using different groups of organisms that use species-and morphology-based approaches for palaeoreconstructions (Korhola and Rautio 2001;Jiang et al. 2019;Cao et al. 2024). Species assemblages of testate amoebae are acknowledged as reliable proxies for a number of environmental factors, such as water temperature (Collins et al. 1990), salinity (Kihlman and Kauppila 2012;Roe and Patterson 2014), pH (Patterson 2000;Escobar et al. 2008), dissolved oxygen (Drljepan et al. 2014), substrate characteristics (Reinhardt et al. 2005) and eutrophication (Laminger 1973). There is no doubt that testate amoebae morphological traits (MT), such as body size (Krashevska et al. 2020) or the presence of outgrowths (Golemansky 2008), are adaptations for the wetness of habitats or sand content in sediments and might be considered a source for providing additional information on their ecological preferences (Céréghino et al. 2018;Fournier et al. 2012). ... > > > > Reference: > > [Morphotrait composition and species structure of benthic lacustrine testate amoebae provide independent information for palaeoreconstructions](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/401272614_Morphotrait_composition_and_species_structure_of_benthic_lacustrine_testate_amoebae_provide_independent_information_for_palaeoreconstructions) [Natural and cultural eutrophication of Sluice Pond, Massachusetts, USA, recorded by algal and protozoan microfossils](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/279954194_Natural_and_cultural_eutrophication_of_Sluice_Pond_Massachusetts_USA_recorded_by_algal_and_protozoan_microfossils?_tp=eyJjb250ZXh0Ijp7InBhZ2UiOiJzY2llbnRpZmljQ29udHJpYnV0aW9ucyIsInByZXZpb3VzUGFnZSI6bnVsbCwic3ViUGFnZSI6bnVsbH19) * Citing Article * [Full-text available](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/279954194_Natural_and_cultural_eutrophication_of_Sluice_Pond_Massachusetts_USA_recorded_by_algal_and_protozoan_microfossils) * December 2014 The Holocene [![Image 50](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/435982309481010/images/template/default/author/author_default_m.jpg)Matea Drljepan](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Matea-Drljepan-2039467270) · [![Image 51](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/276843270819842-1443015987115_Q64/Francine-Mccarthy.jpg)Francine M G Mccarthy](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Francine-Mccarthy) · [![Image 52](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/287888588722183-1445649396139_Q64/J-Hubeny-2.jpg)J. Bradford Hubeny](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/J-Hubeny-2) > ... On the other hand, saprophytic and parasitic NPPs depend on the availability of specific host plants or organic substrates, and their abundance may signal the prevalence of particular plant communities or decomposition levels (Barthelmes et al., 2012;Quamar, 2014). Furthermore, some NPPs are associated with freshwater systems, such as aquatic algae and testate amoebae, serving as sensitive indicators of hydrological regimes, water availability and climatic conditions favourable for aquatic ecosystems (Basumatary et al., 2018;Danesh et al., 2013 Danesh et al., , 2024Mandaokar et al., 2008;Stivrins et al., 2022). Conversely, specific cosmopolitan taxa exhibit broad ecological tolerances, making them markers for detecting broader climatic shifts and environmental stability (Menozzi et al., 2010;Quamar et al., 2024). ... > > > > Reference: > > [Reconstructing mid–late Holocene ecosystem dynamics in the Upper Brahmaputra Valley of Assam, Northeast India, using non-pollen palynomorphs (NPPs)](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/403318622_Reconstructing_mid-late_Holocene_ecosystem_dynamics_in_the_Upper_Brahmaputra_Valley_of_Assam_Northeast_India_using_non-pollen_palynomorphs_NPPs) [Non-pollen Palynomorphs as Indicators of Water Quality in Lake Simcoe, Ontario, Canada](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/271994655_Non-pollen_Palynomorphs_as_Indicators_of_Water_Quality_in_Lake_Simcoe_Ontario_Canada?_tp=eyJjb250ZXh0Ijp7InBhZ2UiOiJzY2llbnRpZmljQ29udHJpYnV0aW9ucyIsInByZXZpb3VzUGFnZSI6bnVsbCwic3ViUGFnZSI6bnVsbH19) * Citing Article * [Full-text available](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/271994655_Non-pollen_Palynomorphs_as_Indicators_of_Water_Quality_in_Lake_Simcoe_Ontario_Canada) * December 2013 Palynology [![Image 53](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/4671872220764/images/template/default/profile/profile_default_m.jpg)Donya Danesh](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Donya-Danesh-2) · [![Image 54](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/276843270819842-1443015987115_Q64/Francine-Mccarthy.jpg)Francine M G Mccarthy](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Francine-Mccarthy) · [![Image 55](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/520405167607809-1501085668524_Q64/Olena-Volik.jpg)Olena Volik](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Olena-Volik) · [![Image 56](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/435982309481010/images/template/default/author/author_default_m.jpg)Matea Drljepan](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Matea-Drljepan-2039467270) > ... We identified regional climate change-driven impacts on the Crawford Lake biosphere over the last 8000 years, since increased effective moisture led to a gradual rise in lake levels throughout mid-latitude eastern North America [69][70][71][72][73]. Warm, moist Middle Holocene/Northgrippian conditions are recorded by abundant pollen of the mesic conifer Tsuga (hemlock) and deciduous trees such as Fagus (beech) in marls, an assemblage resembling that between 270 and 200 cm in the core analysed by Figure 3. Bradford Hubeny](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/J-Hubeny-2) · [![Image 58](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/276843270819842-1443015987115_Q64/Francine-Mccarthy.jpg)Francine M G Mccarthy](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Francine-Mccarthy) · [![Image 59](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/11431281909671761-1770022276337_Q64/Jonathan-Lewis-8.jpg)Jonathan A Lewis](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Jonathan-Lewis-8) · [...] · [![Image 60](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/435982309481010/images/template/default/author/author_default_m.jpg)Mary Lynne Crispo](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Crispo-ML-2065078720) > ... Selenopemphix dionaeacysta, recorded in this study during the late Tortonian (~7.56 Ma) (Fig. 4), was described in deposits from the Zanclean-Tortonian of the Labrador Sea (Head et al., 1989c). This species has been recorded at mid-latitudes of the North Atlantic for the mid Burdigalian (Williams et al., 2004), during the Langhian-Serravallian transition in the equatorial Pacific (Duque-Herrera et al., 2018), and from the Serravallian in the East Antarctic (Bijl et al., 2018), western North Atlantic (De Verteuil and Norris, 1996; J o u r n a l P r e -p r o o f Journal Pre-proof McCarthy et al., 2013), the Gulf of Mexico (Duffield and Stein, 1986), and the Caribbean, to the eastern boundary of the Colombia Basin (Leandro et al., 2020). Concerning its HO, there are no records younger than the late Miocene in tropical areas. ... > > > > Reference: > > [Late Neogene chronostratigraphy and integrated paleoecological trends in the southwestern Caribbean Sea](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/358678407_Late_Neogene_chronostratigraphy_and_integrated_paleoecological_trends_in_the_southwestern_Caribbean_Sea) [from Integrated Ocean Drilling Program Expedition 313 Sea-level control of New Jersey margin architecture: Palynological evidence Sea-level control of New Jersey margin architecture: Palynological evidence from Bradford Hubeny](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/J-Hubeny-2) · [...] · [![Image 4](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/4671872220764/images/template/default/profile/profile_default_m.jpg)Michael Mackinnon](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Michael-Mackinnon-3) Assemblages of dinoflagellate cysts in lakebed sediments across the deep, meromictic basin of Sluice Pond (Lynn, Massachusetts) were compared with measurements of water quality and sediment geochemistry and with testate amoeba and tintinnid assemblages. Lakebed sediments below oxygenated waters (DO >2 mg/L) contain diverse testate amoebae, but sparse Peridinium willei-dominated dinoflagellate cyst assemblages. Sediments collected under hypoxic conditions (DO ... According to qualitative studies, the most diverse cyst assemblages are found in sediments from meso-eutrophic lakes with circumneutral pH (Zippi et al., 1990(Zippi et al., , 1991McCarthy et al., 2011 McCarthy et al., , 2017. A number of studies have been completed using different groups of organisms that use species-and morphology-based approaches for palaeoreconstructions (Korhola and Rautio 2001;Jiang et al. 2019;Cao et al. 2024). Species assemblages of testate amoebae are acknowledged as reliable proxies for a number of environmental factors, such as water temperature (Collins et al. 1990), salinity (Kihlman and Kauppila 2012;Roe and Patterson 2014), pH (Patterson 2000;Escobar et al. 2008), dissolved oxygen (Drljepan et al. 2014), substrate characteristics (Reinhardt et al. 2005) and eutrophication (Laminger 1973). There is no doubt that testate amoebae morphological traits (MT), such as body size (Krashevska et al. 2020) or the presence of outgrowths (Golemansky 2008), are adaptations for the wetness of habitats or sand content in sediments and might be considered a source for providing additional information on their ecological preferences (Céréghino et al. 2018;Fournier et al. 2012). ... > > > > Reference: > > [Morphotrait composition and species structure of benthic lacustrine testate amoebae provide independent information for palaeoreconstructions](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/401272614_Morphotrait_composition_and_species_structure_of_benthic_lacustrine_testate_amoebae_provide_independent_information_for_palaeoreconstructions) [Natural and cultural eutrophication of Sluice Pond, Massachusetts, USA, recorded by algal and protozoan microfossils](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/279954194_Natural_and_cultural_eutrophication_of_Sluice_Pond_Massachusetts_USA_recorded_by_algal_and_protozoan_microfossils?_tp=eyJjb250ZXh0Ijp7InBhZ2UiOiJzY2llbnRpZmljQ29udHJpYnV0aW9ucyIsInByZXZpb3VzUGFnZSI6bnVsbCwic3ViUGFnZSI6bnVsbH19) * Citing Article * [Full-text available](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/279954194_Natural_and_cultural_eutrophication_of_Sluice_Pond_Massachusetts_USA_recorded_by_algal_and_protozoan_microfossils) * December 2014 The Holocene [![Image 50](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/435982309481010/images/template/default/author/author_default_m.jpg)Matea Drljepan](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Matea-Drljepan-2039467270) · [![Image 51](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/276843270819842-1443015987115_Q64/Francine-Mccarthy.jpg)Francine M G Mccarthy](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Francine-Mccarthy) · [![Image 52](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/287888588722183-1445649396139_Q64/J-Hubeny-2.jpg)J. On the other hand, saprophytic and parasitic NPPs depend on the availability of specific host plants or organic substrates, and their abundance may signal the prevalence of particular plant communities or decomposition levels (Barthelmes et al., 2012;Quamar, 2014). Furthermore, some NPPs are associated with freshwater systems, such as aquatic algae and testate amoebae, serving as sensitive indicators of hydrological regimes, water availability and climatic conditions favourable for aquatic ecosystems (Basumatary et al., 2018;Danesh et al., 2013 Danesh et al., , 2024Mandaokar et al., 2008;Stivrins et al., 2022). Conversely, specific cosmopolitan taxa exhibit broad ecological tolerances, making them markers for detecting broader climatic shifts and environmental stability (Menozzi et al., 2010;Quamar et al., 2024). ... > > > > Reference: > > [Reconstructing mid–late Holocene ecosystem dynamics in the Upper Brahmaputra Valley of Assam, Northeast India, using non-pollen palynomorphs (NPPs)](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/403318622_Reconstructing_mid-late_Holocene_ecosystem_dynamics_in_the_Upper_Brahmaputra_Valley_of_Assam_Northeast_India_using_non-pollen_palynomorphs_NPPs) [Non-pollen Palynomorphs as Indicators of Water Quality in Lake Simcoe, Ontario, Canada](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/271994655_Non-pollen_Palynomorphs_as_Indicators_of_Water_Quality_in_Lake_Simcoe_Ontario_Canada?_tp=eyJjb250ZXh0Ijp7InBhZ2UiOiJzY2llbnRpZmljQ29udHJpYnV0aW9ucyIsInByZXZpb3VzUGFnZSI6bnVsbCwic3ViUGFnZSI6bnVsbH19) * Citing Article * [Full-text available](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/271994655_Non-pollen_Palynomorphs_as_Indicators_of_Water_Quality_in_Lake_Simcoe_Ontario_Canada) * December 2013 Palynology [![Image 53](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/4671872220764/images/template/default/profile/profile_default_m.jpg)Donya Danesh](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Donya-Danesh-2) · [![Image 54](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/276843270819842-1443015987115_Q64/Francine-Mccarthy.jpg)Francine M G Mccarthy](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Francine-Mccarthy) · [![Image 55](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/520405167607809-1501085668524_Q64/Olena-Volik.jpg)Olena Volik](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Olena-Volik) · [![Image 56](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/435982309481010/images/template/default/author/author_default_m.jpg)Matea Drljepan](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Matea-Drljepan-2039467270) > ... We identified regional climate change-driven impacts on the Crawford Lake biosphere over the last 8000 years, since increased effective moisture led to a gradual rise in lake levels throughout mid-latitude eastern North America [69][70][71][72][73]. Selenopemphix dionaeacysta, recorded in this study during the late Tortonian (~7.56 Ma) (Fig. 4), was described in deposits from the Zanclean-Tortonian of the Labrador Sea (Head et al., 1989c). This species has been recorded at mid-latitudes of the North Atlantic for the mid Burdigalian (Williams et al., 2004), during the Langhian-Serravallian transition in the equatorial Pacific (Duque-Herrera et al., 2018), and from the Serravallian in the East Antarctic (Bijl et al., 2018), western North Atlantic (De Verteuil and Norris, 1996; J o u r n a l P r e -p r o o f Journal Pre-proof McCarthy et al., 2013), the Gulf of Mexico (Duffield and Stein, 1986), and the Caribbean, to the eastern boundary of the Colombia Basin (Leandro et al., 2020). Borehole data in this work are from IODP Expedition 313 sites M27, M28, and M29, which were drilled 45-65 km offshore New Jersey (Fig. 2). Core and log measurements from the three sites have been used to (1) investigate the amplitudes and mechanisms of eustatic change since the Oligocene Katz et al., 2013;Kotthoff et al., 2014;McCarthy et al., 2013;Expedition 313 Scientists, 2010) and (2) evaluate the sedimentary response of a rifted continental margin to such eustatic changes (Ando et al., 2014;Degirmency, 2014;Gallegos, 2017;Hendra, 2010;Hodgson et al., 2018;Inwood et al., 2013;Lofi et al., 2013;McCarthy et al., 2013;Expedition 313 Scientists, 2010). To go a step forward in advancing the previous findings, we used the following measurements from the three sites: spectral gamma ray (thorium, uranium, and . ... > > > > Reference: > > [Seismic anatomy of Miocene clinoform sequences on the New Jersey (USA) shelf, and implications for sediment transport during base-level rhythms](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/388974070_Seismic_anatomy_of_Miocene_clinoform_sequences_on_the_New_Jersey_USA_shelf_and_implications_for_sediment_transport_during_base-level_rhythms) [Sea-level control of New Jersey margin architecture: Palynological evidence from Integrated Ocean Drilling Program Expedition 313](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/259266666_Sea-level_control_of_New_Jersey_margin_architecture_Palynological_evidence_from_Integrated_Ocean_Drilling_Program_Expedition_313?_tp=eyJjb250ZXh0Ijp7InBhZ2UiOiJzY2llbnRpZmljQ29udHJpYnV0aW9ucyIsInByZXZpb3VzUGFnZSI6bnVsbCwic3ViUGFnZSI6bnVsbH19) * Citing Article * [Full-text available](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/259266666_Sea-level_control_of_New_Jersey_margin_architecture_Palynological_evidence_from_Integrated_Ocean_Drilling_Program_Expedition_313) * October 2013 Geosphere [![Image 65](https://i1.rgstatic.net/ii/profile.image/276843270819842-1443015987115_Q64/Francine-Mccarthy.jpg)Francine M G Mccarthy](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Francine-Mccarthy) · [![Image 66](https://c5.rgstatic.net/m/435982309481010/images/template/default/author/author_default_m.jpg)M. Saturday, May 17, 2025 * * * Following the flooding that occurred from May 12 to 14, LMOA staff collected water samples from Lake Monticello as a precautionary measure. The lab results indicate that elevated E. coli levels were detected in the lake on May 15, at levels expected to be seen after flooding and heavy rain. According to EPA guidelines, the level warrants a swim advisory, not a lake closure. LMOA always recommends waiting at least 48 hours after a storm before swimming in Lake Monticello. Heavy rain washes chemicals, animal feces, and other contaminants from yards into the lake. Please consider this when deciding to enter the water. Also, after flooding, check the water where you will be swimming for foreign objects that may have been swept into the lake that can cause injury. These can be reported to Facilities & Maintenance by using this [FORM.](https://form.jotform.com/243164094579162) Swimming at Lake Monticello is done at one’s own risk; however, LMOA is dedicated to the safety of its residents and takes steps to mitigate risk when possible.The test results have been posted on the member side of this website. ## Untitled SINGLE COLUMN LAYOUT # Comcast Channel 977 Schedule # Comcast Channel 977 Schedule Updated on July 15,2026 ## [CLICK HERE!](https://drive.google.com/file/d/197jXR1iUdIs8NFn_y6sV1nDK1vhqa869/view?usp=sharing) ![Image 2](https://lmoa.org/documents/20124/0/Broadcast-Schedule-1.jpg/8e0615c2-45ce-6a4f-47b8-cb3bbf030b70?t=1701288196945) [![Image 3: Back To Top Button](https://lmoa.org/o/LakeMonticello-theme/images/club-images/back_to_top.png)](javascript:;) [Close menu](https://lmoa.org/web/pages/announcements?announcementId=188&backURL=/news-archive&_jsfBridgeRedirect=true#mm-0)

title: Big Gaston blues will be plenty hungry in July after spawning
fullText: Title: Big Gaston blues will be plenty hungry in July after spawning URL Source: https://www.carolinasportsman.com/featured/big-gaston-blues-will-be-plenty-hungry-in-july-after-spawning/ Published Time: 2018-06-26T10:16:00+00:00 Markdown Content: [MENU](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/featured/big-gaston-blues-will-be-plenty-hungry-in-july-after-spawning/#) [![Image 1](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/ABUGarcia.jpg)](https://classic.avantlink.com/click.php?tool_type=ml&merchant_link_id=d1d07185-d5b8-466c-a42d-0c6658ea7572&website_id=6662ab1f-e488-4067-8603-bb4597b5e6fd) [![Image 2: Carolina Sportsman](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/cropped-IMG_0060.jpeg)](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/) [MENU](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/featured/big-gaston-blues-will-be-plenty-hungry-in-july-after-spawning/#) July 15, 2026 Search for: # Big Gaston blues will be plenty hungry in July after spawning [Dusty Wilson](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/author/dusty-wilson/)[Catfish](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/category/fishing/freshwater-fishing/catfish/), [Featured](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/category/featured/), [Fishing Hotspots](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/category/fishing-hotspots/) ![Image 3: Do big blue cats bite during the summer? Guide Zakk Royce caught this 102-pound Lake Gaston monster in July 2017.](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/06/p1530037521-678x381.jpg) Do big blue cats bite during the summer? Guide Zakk Royce caught this 102-pound Lake Gaston monster in July 2017. While many fish species lay low when summer temperatures skyrocket, blue catfish will be turning up the heat themselves. According to guide Zakk Royce, the blues in Lake Gaston on the North Carolina-Virginia border will be fresh off the spawn this month and in hot pursuit of baitfish on main-lake points and the river channel just above the thermocline. “July is an excellent time to fish for blues of all sizes,” said Royce (252-398-7192), who runs Blues Brothers Catfishing Guide Service. “If I had to pick one time of the year to fish, it would be the middle of summer — just because of how aggressive the fish are. They’re making up for lost time when they haven’t been feeding as much during the spawn. Their metabolism will be way up, and it will make for a really consistent bite.” Royce’s key to summer catfishing is to keep an eye on the thermocline, a spot where cooler and warmer water meet and which is often depicted on a depth finder as a distinct line well above the bottom. Blues typically stay slightly above it because it contains the coolest water that is still well oxygenated. His first approach is to target main-lake points at creek mouths and humps that rise above this depth — 20 feet or less. Mussel beds that provide additional forage in these areas are a major plus. Next, Royce heads to the main-river channel, often choosing areas with a sharp bend or junction with an old creek bed. Although depths here may reach 30 to 40 feet, Royce will suspend his baits at or above the thermocline, which will normally hover in the 15-foot range. “I like to troll (with) slip floats in July,” he said. “One will run off each corner of the stern, and one will run off each side of the boat, spread out by planer boards. I’ll stagger the depths above the thermocline, but if one or two gets hammered consistently, I’ll adjust the others. Two or three feet can make a big difference. Anything between the thermocline and the surface is fair game.” Royce trolls between .5 and 1 mph with cut shad, white perch or bluegill under a 3-inch, round slip float, but he has caught fish going as fast as 2 mph. He uses a 7/0 or 9/0 circle hook, tied to a 60-pound mono leader of 12 to 24 inches. A 3- to 4-ounce egg sinker rides above the swivel tied to 30-pound mono main line. pages.text: Title: Big Gaston blues will be plenty hungry in July after spawning URL Source: https://www.carolinasportsman.com/featured/big-gaston-blues-will-be-plenty-hungry-in-july-after-spawning/ Published Time: 2018-06-26T10:16:00+00:00 Markdown Content: [MENU](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/featured/big-gaston-blues-will-be-plenty-hungry-in-july-after-spawning/#) [![Image 1](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/ABUGarcia.jpg)](https://classic.avantlink.com/click.php?tool_type=ml&merchant_link_id=d1d07185-d5b8-466c-a42d-0c6658ea7572&website_id=6662ab1f-e488-4067-8603-bb4597b5e6fd) [![Image 2: Carolina Sportsman](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/cropped-IMG_0060.jpeg)](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/) [MENU](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/featured/big-gaston-blues-will-be-plenty-hungry-in-july-after-spawning/#) July 15, 2026 Search for: # Big Gaston blues will be plenty hungry in July after spawning [Dusty Wilson](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/author/dusty-wilson/)[Catfish](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/category/fishing/freshwater-fishing/catfish/), [Featured](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/category/featured/), [Fishing Hotspots](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/category/fishing-hotspots/) ![Image 3: Do big blue cats bite during the summer? Guide Zakk Royce caught this 102-pound Lake Gaston monster in July 2017.](https://www.carolinasportsman.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/06/p1530037521-678x381.jpg) Do big blue cats bite during the summer? Guide Zakk Royce caught this 102-pound Lake Gaston monster in July 2017. While many fish species lay low when summer temperatures skyrocket, blue catfish will be turning up the heat themselves. According to guide Zakk Royce, the blues in Lake Gaston on the North Carolina-Virginia border will be fresh off the spawn this month and in hot pursuit of baitfish on main-lake points and the river channel just above the thermocline. “July is an excellent time to fish for blues of all sizes,” said Royce (252-398-7192), who runs Blues Brothers Catfishing Guide Service. “If I had to pick one time of the year to fish, it would be the middle of summer — just because of how aggressive the fish are. They’re making up for lost time when they haven’t been feeding as much during the spawn. Their metabolism will be way up, and it will make for a really consistent bite.” Royce’s key to summer catfishing is to keep an eye on the thermocline, a spot where cooler and warmer water meet and which is often depicted on a depth finder as a distinct line well above the bottom. Blues typically stay slightly above it because it contains the coolest water that is still well oxygenated. His first approach is to target main-lake points at creek mouths and humps that rise above this depth — 20 feet or less. Mussel beds that provide additional forage in these areas are a major plus. Next, Royce heads to the main-river channel, often choosing areas with a sharp bend or junction with an old creek bed. Although depths here may reach 30 to 40 feet, Royce will suspend his baits at or above the thermocline, which will normally hover in the 15-foot range. “I like to troll (with) slip floats in July,” he said. “One will run off each corner of the stern, and one will run off each side of the boat, spread out by planer boards. I’ll stagger the depths above the thermocline, but if one or two gets hammered consistently, I’ll adjust the others. Two or three feet can make a big difference. Anything between the thermocline and the surface is fair game.” Royce trolls between .5 and 1 mph with cut shad, white perch or bluegill under a 3-inch, round slip float, but he has caught fish going as fast as 2 mph. He uses a 7/0 or 9/0 circle hook, tied to a 60-pound mono leader of 12 to 24 inches. A 3- to 4-ounce egg sinker rides above the swivel tied to 30-pound mono main line.
